function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
If GPU procurement costs declined 50%, what would be the implied effect on AI FCF proxy at Q1 2026 run-rates assuming all else equal? Which filing disclosures limit confidence in such an estimate?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about the impact of a 50% decline in GPU procurement costs on an "AI FCF proxy" at Q1 2026 run-rates for SpaceX, and what filing disclosures limit confidence in such an estimate.

This is a complex analytical question that requires me to:

1. Parse the SpaceX S-1 filing
2. Understand the AI segment's financials
3. Identify GPU procurement costs specifically
4. Calculate the FCF proxy for AI segment at Q1 2026 run-rates
5. Model the impact of a 50% decline in GPU costs
6. Identify disclosure limitations

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing to understand the AI segment financials and GPU procurement costs.

steps.reasoning_content:
Good, the SpaceX S-1 filing is now parsed. Let me retrieve information about the AI segment's financials, particularly focusing on GPU procurement costs and free cash flow (FCF) proxy. I'll need to search for:

1. Segment information for AI/Starshield
2. GPU costs or compute infrastructure costs
3. Capital expenditures related to GPU/infrastructure
4. Any FCF proxy or cash flow information

Let me start by searching for relevant sections in the filing.

arguments.prompt: What are the AI segment or xAI segment revenues, operating expenses, capital expenditures, and any GPU or compute infrastructure costs disclosed in {{spaceX_s1}}? Include any segment-level financial data, Adjusted EBITDA, or cash flow information.

results.content:
Based on the provided financial excerpts from the company's filings, here is a consolidated summary of the **AI segment's** key financial metrics, including the **xAI segment** (which became part of the AI segment in February 2026).

### **AI Segment Financial Summary**

#### **1. Revenue**
*   **Q1 2026:** **$818 million**
*   **FY 2025:** **$3,201 million**
*   **FY 2024:** $2,620 million
*   **FY 2023:** $2,961 million

#### **2. Operating Expenses (Costs and Expenses)**
These are broken down as follows:
*   **Q1 2026:** Total costs and expenses of **$3,287 million**.
    *   Cost of revenue: $456 million
    *   Research and development: **$2,379 million** (primarily driven by depreciation of GPU hardware, cloud computing, and data center infrastructure)
    *   Selling, general and administrative: $463 million
    *   Restructuring charges: $(11) million (benefit)
*   **FY 2025:** Total costs and expenses of **$12,990 million** (derived from the operating loss and revenue).
    *   The year-over-year increase in operating loss was primarily driven by **higher cloud computing costs, facilities-related costs, and employee expenses**.

#### **3. Capital Expenditures**
Capital expenditures for the AI segment have grown dramatically to fund compute infrastructure.
*   **Q1 2026:** **$7,723 million**
*   **FY 2025:** **$12,727 million**
*   FY 2024: $5,633 million
*   FY 2023: $463 million

#### **4. GPU & Compute Infrastructure Costs (as part of Operating Expenses)**
*   **Research and Development Expense** is the primary category where GPU depreciation and cloud compute costs are recorded.
    *   **Q1 2026 AI R&D:** **$2,379 million**, a **$1,471 million increase** from Q1 2025, specifically driven by "depreciation of GPU hardware, and the cost of cloud computing and data center infrastructure."
    *   **FY 2025 AI R&D:** The MD&A notes the AI loss from operations increased by $4.79 billion in 2025 due to these costs.
*   The key operational metric for scale is **Nameplate Compute Draw**, which reached **1 gigawatt** in Q1 2026 (up from 0.3 gigawatts in Q1 2025 and 0.8 gigawatts for FY 2025).

#### **5. Segment Adjusted EBITDA (Non-GAAP)**
This is Segment Income (Loss) from Operations plus depreciation/amortization, share-based compensation, restructuring charges, and impairment.
*   **Q1 2026:** **$(609) million**
*   **FY 2025:** **$(1,237) million**
*   FY 2024: $347 million
*   FY 2023: $1,222 million

The company expects a **"multi-year investment horizon"** before AI segment deployments translate into sustained positive Adjusted EBITDA, citing the massive capital investment needed to capture the market opportunity.

#### **Reconciliation of Segment Income (Loss) to Segment Adjusted EBITDA (AI Segment)**
| (in millions) | Q1 2026 | FY 2025 | FY 2024 | FY 2023 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Income (Loss) from Operations** | **$(2,469)** | **$(6,355)** | $(1,561) | $(3,973) |
| Add: Depreciation & Amortization | 1,493 | 3,568 | 1,679 | 1,180 |
| Add: Share-based Compensation | 378 | 1,063 | 16 | 3 |
| Add: Restructuring Charges | (11) | 487 | 213 | 237 |
| Add: Impairment | — | — | — | 3,775 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$(609)** | **$(1,237)** | **$347** | **$1,222** |

**Source:** Data is aggregated from the segment financial tables, capital expenditure tables, and MD&A narrative provided in the excerpts.

steps.reasoning_content:
Excellent! Now I have the AI segment financial data. Let me also retrieve information about GPU procurement costs specifically - I need to understand the breakdown between:
1. GPU depreciation (already in R&D)
2. Cloud computing costs
3. Capital expenditures (which would be GPU procurement)

I also need to understand how GPU procurement costs flow through the financials - are they capitalized and depreciated, or expensed immediately? Let me search for more details on the GPU cost structure and any FCF proxy.

arguments.prompt: What is the breakdown between capital expenditures and operating expenses for GPU and compute infrastructure in the AI segment? Is there any disclosure about GPU procurement costs versus GPU depreciation, cloud computing costs, or data center infrastructure costs in {{spaceX_s1}}? Include any information about how compute costs are treated (capitalized vs expensed).

results.content:
# GPU and Compute Infrastructure Cost Breakdown

## Direct Financial Disclosures

The documents provide meaningful but **incomplete** detail on how compute costs flow between CapEx and OpEx. Here's what can be pieced together:

---

## Capital Expenditures (AI Segment)

| Period | AI CapEx |
|---|---|
| FY 2023 | $463M |
| FY 2024 | $5,633M |
| Q1 2025 | $2,567M |

The explosive growth from $463M to $5,633M in a single year signals a massive GPU procurement and data center buildout. The documents confirm CapEx covers:

- **GPU/processor procurement** (the largest component)
- **Servers and networking equipment** (listed as a PP&E category)
- **Data center infrastructure** (also a separate PP&E line item)
- **Construction-in-progress** for facilities not yet in service
- **Capitalized interest** ($169M in FY2025, $7M in Q1 2026) on AI data center construction

---

## Operating Expenses — What Hits the P&L

### GPU Depreciation (Capitalized → Expensed)

Once GPUs and infrastructure are placed in service, they depreciate into **R&D expense**:

| Period | GPU Depreciation in R&D |
|---|---|
| Q1 2026 vs Q1 2025 | +$908M increase |
| FY 2025 vs FY 2024 | +$1,673M increase |

The FY 2025 AI segment total depreciation and amortization was **$1,679M** (from the supplemental segment table), nearly all of which appears tied to compute hardware.

### Cloud Computing and Data Center Operating Costs

These hit **R&D** as period expenses:

| Period | Cloud/Data Center OpEx Increase (in R&D) |
|---|---|
| Q1 2026 vs Q1 2025 | +$301M |
| FY 2025 vs FY 2024 | +$1,440M |

Additionally, **cost of revenue** for AI included infrastructure and cloud computing costs:
- FY 2025 vs FY 2024: +$491M increase in AI cost of revenue from "infrastructure and cloud computing costs"

### Total AI R&D Composition

The documents explicitly list what's inside AI R&D:

> *"cloud computing expenses, employee compensation expenses (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation), power generation costs, and depreciation of data center assets, including processors, equipment lease expenses, and networking equipment."*

For FY 2025, total AI R&D was **$5,064M**, comprising:

| Component | Estimated FY 2025 |
|---|---|
| GPU depreciation | ~$1,673M (incremental) + base ≈ **$1,800M+** |
| Cloud computing & data center infrastructure | ~$1,440M (incremental) + base ≈ **$1,600M+** |
| Employee compensation + overhead | ~$775M (incremental) + base ≈ **$900M+** |
| Power, leases, other | Remainder |

*(These are rough approximations by summing incremental changes to prior year base of $1,176M)*

---

## What Is **Not** Separately Disclosed

The documents do **not** provide:

1. **A standalone "GPU procurement cost" line item** — GPU purchases are buried within total AI CapEx alongside data center construction, networking, and facilities.

2. **The 30/70 split applied to this company's own CapEx** — The SemiAnalysis industry benchmark (30% data center construction, 70% processors/equipment) is cited as general industry data, **not** as this company's own ratio.

3. **Cloud computing costs broken out between third-party cloud (e.g., AWS/GCP/Azure) vs. owned data center operating costs** — Both are lumped together.

4. **A clear CapEx-to-depreciation reconciliation** — While you can see CapEx of $5,633M in FY2024 and total AI D&A of $1,679M in FY2025, the documents don't provide a PP&E rollforward specific to the AI segment or the useful life assumptions for GPUs.

5. **Power costs separately** — Listed as a component of R&D but never quantified independently.

---

## How Compute Costs Are Treated: Capitalized vs. Expensed

The accounting treatment follows this framework:

```
CAPITALIZED (Balance Sheet → CapEx)
├── GPU hardware procurement
├── Servers and networking equipment
├── Data center construction (shell, MEP, power)
├── Construction-in-progress (not yet in service)
└── Capitalized interest during construction

EXPENSED (P&L — R&D)
├── GPU depreciation (once placed in service)
├── Cloud computing expenses (third-party)
├── Power/energy costs to run processors
├── Equipment lease expenses
└── Employee compensation for AI R&D

EXPENSED (P&L — Cost of Revenue)
└── Infrastructure and cloud computing costs
    related to delivering AI products/services
```

---

## The Key Insight: Segmented Economics

The supplemental segment tables reveal the dramatic scaling:

| Metric | AI Segment 2023 | AI Segment 2024 | AI Segment Q1 2025 | AI Segment Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue | $2,961M | $2,620M | $727M | ~$818M* |
| CapEx | $463M | $5,633M | $2,567M | Not disclosed |
| D&A | $1,180M | $1,679M | $771M | Not disclosed |
| R&D | $186M | $1,176M | $908M | $2,379M |
| Op Loss | $(3,973M) | $(1,561M) | $(936M) | $(2,469M) |

*Revenue estimated from the $91M YoY increase cited in the MD&A.

The trajectory shows CapEx **accelerating far ahead of depreciation**, meaning a growing base of compute assets is being built but hasn't yet fully flowed through to the P&L. As these assets depreciate (typically over 3-5 years for server hardware), the depreciation component of R&D will continue climbing — a significant headwind the company warns will persist for a **"multi-year investment horizon"** before reaching sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA.

steps.reasoning_content: This is very helpful. Now I need to understand what an "AI FCF proxy" might be. Let me search for any cash flow information, and also understand the capital expenditure breakdown for Q1 2026 specifically.

arguments.prompt: What is the capital expenditure for AI segment in Q1 2026? Also, is there any disclosure of free cash flow or FCF proxy for the AI segment? Look for any cash flow from operations data for the AI segment, or any non-GAAP measure that approximates FCF for AI in {{spaceX_s1}}.

results.content:
Based on the provided financial documents, here are the answers to your questions:

**1. Capital Expenditure for the AI Segment in Q1 2026:**
The capital expenditure for the AI segment for the three months ended March 31, 2026, was **$7,723 million**.

**2. Disclosure of Free Cash Flow (FCF) or FCF Proxy for the AI Segment:**
There is **no disclosure of free cash flow or a direct FCF proxy** for the AI segment.

*   **Cash Flow from Operations** is reported only on a consolidated basis for the entire company, not broken down by segment.
*   The primary non-GAAP measure used to evaluate the AI segment's performance is **Segment Adjusted EBITDA**. This is described as a non-GAAP financial measure reconciled to the segment's GAAP "income (loss) from operations," which is the most directly comparable GAAP measure.
*   For context, the AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA was a loss of **$(609) million** for Q1 2026 and **$(1,237) million** for the full year 2025.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a clearer picture. The filing doesn't provide a direct FCF proxy for the AI segment, but I can construct one using available data. Let me search for more information about how GPU costs specifically flow through, and whether there's any way to isolate GPU procurement costs from total CapEx.

arguments.prompt: What is the breakdown of capital expenditures between GPU/processor purchases and data center construction for the AI segment? Is there any disclosure about the useful life of GPU assets, or the ratio of hardware to construction costs? Also, is there any information about cloud computing costs versus owned infrastructure costs in {{spaceX_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided financial disclosures, here is a detailed breakdown of your questions:

### 1. Breakdown of Capital Expenditures: GPU/Processors vs. Data Center Construction
The filings provide a **clear, though not directly segmented, breakdown** that allows for a precise calculation.

*   **Industry Standard Cited:** The Preamble explicitly states that for most AI companies, the total capital cost of building compute infrastructure derives approximately **30% from data center construction** and **70% from procuring processors and critical IT equipment** (SemiAnalysis).
*   **Asset Categories in PP&E:** Note 5 lists two primary categories relevant to this split:
    *   **Servers and networking equipment:** This includes processors (GPUs) and other IT hardware.
    *   **Data center infrastructure:** This includes the physical shell, mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP), and grid interconnection.
*   **Calculation for the AI Segment:**
    Using the total AI segment capital expenditures for the year ended December 31, 2025 (**$12,727 million**), and applying the cited 70/30 split:
    *   **Estimated GPU/Processor Purchases (70%):** ~**$8,909 million**
    *   **Estimated Data Center Construction Costs (30%):** ~**$3,818 million**

*   **Supporting Evidence:** The MD&A notes that increases in AI R&D expense were driven by "depreciation of GPU hardware" and "cloud computing and data center infrastructure expenses." Furthermore, **capitalized interest of $169 million** in 2025 was specifically attributed to AI infrastructure data centers, indicating ongoing construction of owned facilities.

### 2. Useful Life of GPU Assets
Yes, there is explicit disclosure. In the **significant accounting policies** note, the estimated useful life for assets containing GPUs is stated:

*   **Servers and networking equipment:** **5 - 6 years**

This category encompasses the processors and GPUs used for AI training and inference.

### 3. Cloud Computing Costs vs. Owned Infrastructure Costs
The disclosures indicate the company uses a **hybrid model of both third-party cloud computing and owned infrastructure**, but a precise ratio is **not explicitly provided**.

*   **Cloud Computing:** The MD&A consistently lists **"cloud computing expenses"** as a component of AI segment R&D and cost of revenue. This represents ongoing operational costs for renting compute capacity from third-party providers.
*   **Owned Infrastructure:** The massive and growing balances in **"Servers and networking equipment"** ($23.9B as of March 2026) and **"Construction-in-progress"** ($14.0B as of March 2026, primarily for AI infrastructure) signify heavy investment in owned hardware and data centers. The capitalized interest disclosure reinforces this.
*   **Conclusion:** The financial statements clearly show the company is both spending on **cloud computing services (OpEx)** and making **substantial capital expenditures (CapEx)** to build out its own infrastructure. The text does not break down the ratio between the two, but the scale of CapEx suggests a strategic focus on controlling long-term compute costs through owned assets.

**In summary:** For the AI segment, the disclosed capital expenditures align with a ~70/30 hardware-to-construction split, GPU assets have a 5-6 year useful life, and the company employs a hybrid compute strategy involving both cloud services and significant owned infrastructure investment.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me construct an "AI FCF proxy" and model the impact of a 50% decline in GPU procurement costs. 

An FCF proxy for the AI segment would typically be:
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA (or Operating Income)
- Less: Capital expenditures
- Plus/minus working capital changes (not disclosed at segment level)

For Q1 2026:
- AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $(609) million
- AI Segment CapEx: $7,723 million

So a basic FCF proxy would be approximately: $(609) million - $7,723 million = $(8,332) million

Now, if GPU procurement costs decline 50%, I need to understand:
1. What portion of CapEx is GPU procurement (approximately 70%)
2. What portion of operating expenses (R&D) is GPU depreciation (which comes from prior CapEx)

Let me calculate the impact:

**Impact on CapEx (direct):**
- Q1 2026 AI CapEx: $7,723 million
- GPU procurement portion (70%): ~$5,406 million
- 50% reduction in GPU procurement: -$2,703 million
- New AI CapEx: $7,723 - $2,703 = $5,020 million

**Impact on R&D (through depreciation):**
This is more complex. The depreciation expense in Q1 2026 reflects GPUs purchased in prior periods. A 50% reduction in current procurement costs would not immediately affect Q1 2026 depreciation - it would affect future periods.

However, the question asks about "run-rates" which implies a forward-looking estimate. Let me think about this more carefully.

Actually, let me re-read the question: "what would be the implied effect on AI FCF proxy at Q1 2026 run-rates assuming all else equal?"

This seems to be asking: if we take Q1 2026 as a run-rate and GPU procurement costs decline 50%, what would the FCF proxy be?


I need to verify the AI segment CapEx figures and calculate the GPU depreciation component more precisely.

arguments.prompt: What are the exact capital expenditure figures for the AI segment for each period presented - FY2023, FY2024, FY2025, and Q1 2026? Also, what is the exact depreciation and amortization figure for the AI segment for Q1 2026? Look for segment-level capital expenditure tables in {{spaceX_s1}}.

results.content:
Based on the provided financial tables in the Preamble section, here are the exact figures you requested:

### **Capital Expenditures for the AI Segment (in millions):**

| Period             | Capital Expenditures (AI Segment) |
| ------------------ | --------------------------------- |
| **FY 2023**        | $463                              |
| **FY 2024**        | $5,633                            |
| **FY 2025**        | $12,727                           |
| **Q1 2026** (Three Months Ended March 31, 2026) | $7,723 |

### 1. AI Segment Q1 2026 Operating Loss and Revenue

arguments.prompt: What is the disclosure about the SemiAnalysis 70/30 split for GPU costs versus data center construction? Is this stated as a company-specific figure or as an industry benchmark? Also, is there any company-specific disclosure about what portion of AI segment CapEx is GPU procurement versus data center construction in {{spaceX_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the S-1 filing, here's a breakdown of the disclosure regarding the SemiAnalysis 70/30 split and the company's own cost structure:

### 1. **The SemiAnalysis 70/30 Split: An Industry Benchmark**
The disclosure explicitly states that the 70/30 split is an **industry benchmark** cited from the research firm SemiAnalysis, **not a company-specific figure**.

*   **Direct Quote:** *"According to SemiAnalysis, for most AI companies without a build cost advantage, their total capital cost of building compute infrastructure derives approximately 30% from data center construction costs (including, but not limited to, the shell; mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (“MEP”); and grid interconnection) and approximately 70% from the cost of procuring processors and critical IT equipment."*
*   **Context:** This benchmark is used in the **Preamble** to establish the general economics of the AI compute industry and to set the stage for the company to highlight its own competitive advantages.

### 2. **Company-Specific Disclosures on GPU vs. Data Center Costs**
While the filing does not provide a specific percentage breakdown for its own GPU procurement versus data center construction costs (like the 70/30 benchmark), it provides **substantial qualitative and some quantitative disclosures** that illustrate its cost advantages and capital intensity.

**Key Company-Specific Points:**

*   **Capital Intensity of AI Segment:** The AI segment requires massive capital investment. The company reports an accumulated deficit of **$41.3 billion** as of March 31, 2026, and states it expects "a multi-year investment horizon" before achieving sustained positive Adjusted EBITDA in the AI segment.
*   **Focus on Cost Efficiency vs. Industry Benchmarks:** The company repeatedly claims its data center construction costs are **"considerably lower than industry benchmarks on a per megawatt basis."** It uses the rapid deployment times of COLOSSUS (122 days) and COLOSSUS II (91 days) compared to an industry benchmark of **~2 years** for a 100MW greenfield data center as proof of its speed and cost advantage.
*   **Expense Drivers:** Financial statements confirm the dual cost drivers.
    *   **GPU/Hardware Costs:** Highlighted as a major expense. Research & Development expense for the AI segment increased by **$1,471 million** in Q1 2026, driven primarily by **"depreciation of GPU hardware."**
    *   **Data Center Construction Costs:** Disclosed as "construction-in-progress" for "AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service." Depreciation of these assets is a significant and growing line item (e.g., **$5,915 million** for 2025).
*   **Vertical Integration Strategy:** The filing positions the company's vertical integration (e.g., the **Terafab** initiative with Tesla to manufacture compute hardware) as a direct strategy to reduce future "compute hardware costs" and alleviate supply chain issues. This indicates a focus on controlling the 70% cost component identified in the benchmark.

### Summary
| Disclosure Type | Content | Source/Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Industry Benchmark** | **70% processors/IT, 30% data center construction** | Cited as an **industry-wide figure** from SemiAnalysis in the Preamble. |
| **Company-Specific** | **Costs are "considerably lower" per MW** than industry benchmarks. | **Preamble:** Claimed competitive advantage for COLOSSUS II. |
| **Company-Specific** | **Dual Cost Drivers:** Significant expenses for **GPU depreciation** and **data center construction-in-progress.** | **MD&A & Financial Statements:** Describes the primary components of AI segment losses and capital intensity. |
| **Company-Specific** | **Vertical Integration (Terafab)** aims to reduce **compute hardware costs.** | **Preamble:** Strategy to control a major portion of the cost structure. |

**Conclusion:** The 70/30 split is presented as a **general industry benchmark**. The company does not provide its own specific percentage ratio but demonstrates through operational claims (speed, cost per MW) and financial disclosures (massive depreciation of GPUs and construction-in-progress) that it is aggressively managing both sides of this cost equation, with a particular strategic emphasis on controlling hardware costs through vertical integration.

arguments.final_result:
## Analysis: 50% GPU Procurement Cost Decline Impact on AI FCF Proxy

### 1. Constructing the AI FCF Proxy at Q1 2026 Run-Rates

The SpaceX S-1 does **not** disclose a segment-level free cash flow (FCF) measure for the AI segment. I constructed an FCF proxy using disclosed non-GAAP and GAAP measures:

**Primary FCF Proxy Formula:**
```
AI FCF Proxy = Segment Adjusted EBITDA − Segment Capital Expenditures
```

**Q1 2026 AI Segment Data (as reported):**
| Metric | Q1 2026 |
|--------|---------|
| AI Segment Revenue | $818 million |
| AI Segment Operating Loss | $(2,469) million |
| AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $(609) million |
| AI Segment Depreciation & Amortization | $1,493 million |
| AI Segment Capital Expenditures | $7,723 million |
| AI Segment R&D Expense | $2,379 million |

**Baseline AI FCF Proxy:**
```
$(609) − $7,723 = $(8,332) million
```

---

### 2. Modeling the 50% GPU Procurement Cost Decline

**Assumption Framework:**
The S-1 cites a SemiAnalysis industry benchmark indicating that **~70% of AI compute infrastructure CapEx** derives from "procuring processors and critical IT equipment" (GPUs), with ~30% from data center construction. **Important caveat:** This is presented as an *industry-wide benchmark*, not a SpaceX-specific ratio. The company does not disclose its own GPU-to-construction CapEx split.

**CapEx Adjustment Calculation:**

| Component | Calculation | Amount |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Q1 2026 AI CapEx | Reported | $7,723.00 million |
| GPU procurement portion | $7,723 × 70% | $5,406.10 million |
| 50% cost reduction | $5,406.10 × 50% | $(2,703.05) million |
| **Adjusted AI CapEx** | $7,723 − $2,703.05 | **$5,019.95 million** |

**Adjusted AI FCF Proxy:**
```
$(609) − $5,019.95 = $(5,628.95) million
```

---

### 3. Implied FCF Impact Summary

| Metric | Baseline | Adjusted (−50% GPU Cost) | Change |
|--------|----------|--------------------------|--------|
| AI Segment CapEx | $7,723.00 million | $5,019.95 million | $(2,703.05) million |
| AI FCF Proxy | $(8,332.00) million | $(5,628.95) million | **+$2,703.05 million** |
| % Improvement | — | — | **32.4%** |

A 50% decline in GPU procurement costs would **improve the AI FCF proxy by approximately $2.70 billion** at Q1 2026 run-rates, reducing the quarterly cash burn from ~$8.3 billion to ~$5.6 billion—a **32.4% improvement** in the FCF deficit.

---

### 4. Depreciation Considerations (Forward-Looking Caveat)

The above analysis holds Q1 2026 depreciation ($1,493 million) constant, as depreciation reflects GPUs *previously purchased* at higher costs. However:

- GPU assets are depreciated over **5–6 years** (per the accounting policy note)
- GPUs procured at 50% lower costs would generate proportionally lower depreciation in future periods
- The Q1 2026 R&D increase of $1,471 million year-over-year was driven by $908 million in additional GPU depreciation
- Future periods would see a compounding benefit as cheaper GPUs flow through to the P&L

This forward-looking depreciation tailwind is **not captured** in the Q1 2026 run-rate analysis but would be material over a 5–6 year horizon.

---

### 5. Filing Disclosures That Limit Confidence in This Estimate

**Critical Limitations:**

| Limitation | Impact on Estimate Confidence |
|------------|-------------------------------|
| **1. No company-specific GPU/CapEx split disclosed** | The 70/30 ratio is an *industry benchmark* from SemiAnalysis, not SpaceX's own disclosure. SpaceX claims its data center construction costs are "considerably lower than industry benchmarks on a per megawatt basis," suggesting its ratio may differ materially from 70/30. The company's vertical integration strategy (Terafab) could further alter this ratio. |
| **2. No segment-level cash flow from operations** | The FCF proxy uses Adjusted EBITDA as a starting point, but this excludes working capital changes, cash taxes, and other operating cash flow items that could be material. |
| **3. Cloud computing costs not separated from owned infrastructure** | The filing lumps "cloud computing expenses" (third-party) with depreciation of owned GPU assets. A 50% decline in GPU procurement costs might not reduce third-party cloud rental rates proportionally, or might shift the cloud/owned mix. |
| **4. R&D composition undisclosed** | The $2,379 million Q1 2026 R&D includes GPU depreciation, cloud computing, employee compensation, power costs, and equipment leases—but no itemized breakdown is provided. |
| **5. Historical periods reflect recast consolidated entity** | All financials include xAI (merged Feb 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged March 28, 2025) under common-control accounting. Segment figures do not reflect standalone SpaceX AI operations exclusively. |
| **6. Construction-in-progress timing uncertain** | As of Q1 2026, $14.0 billion in construction-in-progress exists (primarily for AI). Future depreciation expense depends on when these assets are placed in service, which is not disclosed. |
| **7. Capitalized interest assumptions** | The company capitalized $7 million in interest in Q1 2026 ($169 million in FY2025) for AI data centers. Changes in GPU costs could affect project scope and capitalized interest. |
| **8. No disclosure on GPU useful life sensitivity** | If cheaper GPUs have different performance characteristics, useful life assumptions might change, affecting depreciation expense beyond a simple cost reduction. |

---

### 6. Conclusion

**Direct Answer:** A 50% decline in GPU procurement costs would improve the AI FCF proxy by approximately **$2.70 billion (32.4%)** at Q1 2026 run-rates, reducing the quarterly cash burn from ~$8.3 billion to ~$5.6 billion, assuming all else equal.

**Confidence Level:** **Low-to-Moderate.** The estimate is highly sensitive to the undisclosed GPU-to-construction CapEx ratio. The 70% assumption is externally sourced and may not reflect SpaceX's vertically integrated cost structure. Furthermore, the absence of segment-level cash flow data, R&D composition, and cloud cost breakdowns prevents precise modeling. The filing explicitly warns of a "multi-year investment horizon" before positive AI Adjusted EBITDA, suggesting GPU costs are one of several material variables affecting near-term FCF.
